feed.title: Get Lost. Then find your way back. - Python
feed.subtitle: Explore everything

entry.title: Indico Server on OSX
entry.summary:
Prereqs

OSX is already in a good position to run Indico. A few modifications needs to be made:

  • Install ModWSGI:
  • {{ post_url modwsgi }}
  • Setup SSL on the Apache Server:
  • {{ post_url 2015-02-03-ssl-keys-on-osx }}
  • flask engine plugin
  • from git

The next step is to setup PostgreSQL. Annoyingly enough, this is never mentioned in the …
entry.content:
Prereqs

OSX is already in a good position to run Indico. A few modifications needs to be made:

  • Install ModWSGI:
  • {{ post_url modwsgi }}
  • Setup SSL on the Apache Server:
  • {{ post_url 2015-02-03-ssl-keys-on-osx }}
  • flask engine plugin
  • from git

The next step is to setup PostgreSQL. Annoyingly enough, this is never mentioned in the documentation and they never say to set up the database, which I'll talk about later.

The following changes will be needed:

  • add use _www with full privilages (no password)
  • This may be a little unnecessary
  • add database indico
  • This will be intiialized later

Failing to do this will result in an error message about not being connect to port 5432.

Setup nodeenv

git clone https://github.com/ekalinin/nodeenv.git
cd nodeenv
python setup.py install

Setup the dependencies (these will mostly be js objects which, if missing manifest as) by using Fabric.

'css/lib/angular.css' not found in load path: ['/opt/indico/htdocs', '/opt/indico/htdocs/css', '/opt/indico/htdocs/js']

Fabric will also need to be installed:

    easy_install fabric

Download and install

The source code for indico can be foud on github. I'd download to a source directory directly:

git clone https://github.com/indico/indico

Run Fabric:

fab setup_deps

Run Install (which you'll probably have to do as sudo):

python setup.py install

Usually something will go wrong and a python package will be missing, so the preceding command will be need to run alternatively with this one:

sudo easy_install <module name>

This will typically manifast as the incorrect version of psjson or something, you can use the == operator in easy install to specify the version.

Run the indico configuration::

indico_easy_install

At this point, some of the permissions have to be setup in order for apache to run it

sudo chown -R _www /opt/indico
sudo chgrp -R _www /opt/indico

In order to get some of the site stuff working correctly (and depending on who else uses the machine), the folder at /Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/<some package>

I followed all of the default configurations. At the end of which, I had to change the username in zdctl.conf to _www. This is an OSX specific change.

The following httpd configuration will need to be inserted into the webserver configuration path

AddDefaultCharset UTF-8

<VirtualHost *:80>
        # mod_wsgi indico

    ErrorLog /var/log/apache2/error.log
        LogLevel warn

    Alias /indico/images "/opt/indico/htdocs/images"
    Alias /indico/css "/opt/indico/htdocs/css"
    Alias /indico/js "/opt/indico/htdocs/js"
    Alias /indico/ihelp "/opt/indico/htdocs/ihelp"

    WSGIDaemonProcess WSGIDAEMON processes=32 threads=1 inactivity-timeout=3600 maximum-requests=10000 \
            python-eggs=/opt/indico/tmp/egg-cache

    WSGIScriptAlias /indico "/opt/indico/htdocs/indico.wsgi"

    <Directory "/opt/indico">
        WSGIProcessGroup WSGIDAEMON
        WSGIApplicationGroup %{GLOBAL}
        AllowOverride FollowSymlinks
        Options None
        Order deny,allow
        Allow from all
    </Directory>
</VirtualHost>

<VirtualHost *:443>

    ErrorLog /var/log/apache2/error.log
            LogLevel warn

    Alias /indico/images "/opt/indico/htdocs/images"
    Alias /indico/css "/opt/indico/htdocs/css"
    Alias /indico/js "/opt/indico/htdocs/js"
    Alias /indico/ihelp "/opt/indico/htdocs/ihelp"

    WSGIScriptAlias /indico "/opt/indico/htdocs/indico.wsgi"

    SSLEngine on
    SSLCertificateFile    /etc/apache2/ssl/server.crt
    SSLCertificateKeyFile /etc/apache2/ssl/host.key
</VirtualHost>

The major change was in the FollowSymlinks command. This is due to default behavior in OSX's apache.

In addition, the config file at /opt/indico/etc/indico.conf will need to have the following changes:

-#Debug=False
+Debug=True
+BaseURL              = "http://physics00158.dhcp.unc.edu/indico"

 # BaseSecureURL is the HTTPS location of Indico. Set empty string if you want to use http.                                                                     

+BaseSecureURL        = "https://physics00158.dhcp.unc.edu/indico"
-BaseURL              = "http://localhost:8000"

 # BaseSecureURL is the HTTPS location of Indico. Set empty string if you want to use http.                                                                     

-BaseSecureURL        = "https://localhost:8443"

If you keep these default settings, you can test out indico by running the command:

sudo -u _www indico runserver

Initialize the database:

indico db prepare

Failing to do this will result in an error message about relations missing.

Managing Indico

There's plenty of documentation online. However, there's a few things you should know:

  • You can manually add a user with the following command: 
sudo -u _www indico admin user_add
  • You can grant users admin privilages with the following command:
sudo -u _www indico admin user_grant <user_id>

* The `user_id` is an unsigned integer assigned on user creation. The first user will be 0, etc...

There's also a ton of information here.

entry.title: ModWSGI on OSX
entry.summary:
The source code was found (here)[https://code.google.com/p/modwsgi/wiki/DownloadTheSoftware?tm=2]

{% blockquote %} DO NOT FOLLOW THE INSTRUCTIONS.

Just unload it to a source directory and install it to the apache module directory

Now add the following line to /etc/apache2/httpd.conf

LoadModule wsgi_module modules …
entry.content:
The source code was found (here)[https://code.google.com/p/modwsgi/wiki/DownloadTheSoftware?tm=2]

{% blockquote %} DO NOT FOLLOW THE INSTRUCTIONS.

Just unload it to a source directory and install it to the apache module directory

Now add the following line to /etc/apache2/httpd.conf

LoadModule wsgi_module modules/mod_wsgi.so

And restart the apache server

    apachectl restart

entry.title: Using Sphinx for Inline Documentation
entry.summary:
Brief History

Previously, in my python based endeavors, I've used doxygen, an inline code documentation engine for generating web pages and pdfs. Many people recommend this for c++ API documentation, and in fact, I use it my self all the time.However, to my eternal consternation, it does not play …
entry.content:
Brief History

Previously, in my python based endeavors, I've used doxygen, an inline code documentation engine for generating web pages and pdfs. Many people recommend this for c++ API documentation, and in fact, I use it my self all the time.However, to my eternal consternation, it does not play well with python. I've used various little tricks and hacks to get around this, but a more streamlined approach would be nice. Eventually, I'd like to use something that works with multiple languages, but I guess today is just not that day.

In Comes Sphinx

A brief google search shows that python documentation is almost universally built with sphinx. I say "almost universally" because all the python-standard libraries are covered in sphinx as well as some private libraries, but many other non-standard libraries prefer their own documentation. Older libraries in particular like to use unique approaches.

Sphinx can be very easily researched online, downloaded via yum or apt, and can be used with a few simple to get up and going. However, very few real examples out there detail the whole workflow beginning to end. I'm going to attempt that here.

An Example with Cards

I built a simple simulation in python a while back to prove a point about the number of times it take to shuffle a deck of cards before patterns in the initial deck stop appearing in the shuffled deck (it's 10). Initially, I had the project documented in doxygen, but later switched to sphinx. We'll use this for my example.

Alright, so the project directory initially looks something like this:

  • Cards/
  • Cards/
    • card.py
    • deck.py
    • shuffle.py
    • randomize.py
    • common.py
    • analysis.py
  • scripts/
    • runsim.py
  • tests/
    • testdef.py
    • testshuffle.py
    • testrandom.py

The top level is for the project. The nested directory of the same name (Cards/Cards) is the source directory containing python modules for the project. I'm treating this directory as a module in order to make it simple for sphinx, so I've included the __init__.py file with the __all__=[] directive to tell sphinx which files are a part of the directory. This is also the directory that get installed when the project goes to release.

The directory scripts/ is for python scripts that utilizes Cards. Likewise tests/ is for unit tests. This is a bit overkill for such a narrow project, but it's good practice anyway.

Installation

Now, I'm going to put a directory /doc in the structure, so that it looks like this:

  • Cards/
  • Cards/
    • card.py
    • deck.py
    • shuffle.py
    • randomize.py
    • common.py
    • analysis.py
  • scripts/
    • runsim.py
  • tests/
    • testdef.py
    • testshuffle.py
    • testrandom.py
  • doc/

This will house the new documentation code. cd into the new doc directory

cd doc

Then run the following commands:

sphinx-quickstart
sphinx-apidoc -o source/ ../Cards

Quickstart starts the process of making the build system for the documentation. The steps are easy to go through, and self-explanatory. Meanwhile apidoc searches the code for symbols and adds them automatically to documentation files.

apidoc will need to be rerun every time you add new classes and symbols to the project.

Now, the directories should look this:

  • Cards/
  • Cards/
    • card.py
    • deck.py
    • shuffle.py
    • randomize.py
    • common.py
    • analysis.py
  • scripts/
    • runsim.py
  • tests/
    • testdef.py
    • testshuffle.py
    • testrandom.py
  • doc/
    • source/
      • _static/
      • _templates/
      • index.rst
      • Cards.rst
      • modules.rst
      • conf.py
    • build/
    • Makefile
    • make.bat

Making it Github Ready

Git is being used for this project, and I'd like to deploy to gh-pages, if at all necessary, so, we're going to need to add in a .gitignore file. to Cards/ with the contents:

*.pyc
*.po
doc/build

The first two are because inevitably, some code will be compiled in the source tree and we want to keep it clean for distribution and the last one is because we're going to make doc/build a git repo for the gh-pages branch. You can now initialize the two repositories via the following commands:

cd ..
git init
git add .
git commit -m "first commit"
git remote add origin https://github.com/<your username>/<project name>.git
git push -u origin master
cd doc/build
mkdir html
git clone https://github.com/<your username>/<project name>.git html
cd html
git branch -b gh-pages
git rm -rf *
cd ../..
make
cd build/html
git commit -a -m "First Documentation commit"
git push

You should have your project and it's github pages up and ready to go. To commit your code (from Cards/):

git commit -a -m "Your commit message"
git push

To push your documenation:

cd doc
make
cd build/html
git commit -a -m "Documentation commit message"
git push

Page Creation

Unlike doxygen, pages will not be automatically created for EVERYTHING. apidoc will have taken care of some of that, but the rest is a bit verbose. matplotlib has some tools for getting around that, but for sily little projects like this, we may need something less involved.

The first page generated is going to be index.rst. This is a pretty good example of how to structure pages. Namely, pages that are going to be referenced as the first line:

Your Title
==========

And they need a table of contents(toc) reference: 

.. toctree::
   :maxdepth: 4

If you want to add the API code to the table of contents, do the following:

.. toctree::
   :maxdepth: 4
   modules

Add your own pages in the same way to create more docs.

Module Documentation

{% blockquote %} Sphinx extracts docstrings from the files themselves, so if you're already following python documentation procedure, then skip forward to symbolic reference.

For example, for the module Cards.Card, the doc string is:

"""Example Google style docstrings.

This module demonstrates documentation as specified by the `Google Python
Style Guide`_. Docstrings may extend over multiple lines. Sections are created
with a section header and a colon followed by a block of indented text.

Example:
  Examples can be given using either the ``Example`` or ``Examples``
  sections. Sections support any reStructuredText formatting, including
  literal blocks::

      $ python Card.py

Section breaks are created by simply resuming unindented text. Section breaks
are also implicitly created anytime a new section starts.

Attributes:
  module_level_variable (int): Module level variables may be documented in
    either the ``Attributes`` section of the module docstring, or in an
    inline docstring immediately following the variable.

    Either form is acceptable, but the two should not be mixed. Choose
    one convention to document module level variables and be consistent
    with it.

.. _Google Python Style Guide:
   http://google-styleguide.googlecode.com/svn/trunk/pyguide.html

"""

You can in notes and warnings seemingly where you want. I tend to put them in modules, classes and functions that need them.

Class Documentation

Classes are documented after the declaration statement. For example:

class Card:
    """Class are documented after the declaration statement.

    The __init__ method may be documented in either the class level
    docstring, or as a docstring on the __init__ method itself.

    Either form is acceptable, but the two should not be mixed. Choose one
    convention to document the __init__ method and be consistent with it.

    Note:
      Do not include the `self` parameter in the ``Args`` section.

    Args:
      msg (str): Human readable string describing the exception.
      code (int, optional): Error code, defaults to 2.

    Attributes:
      msg (str): Human readable string describing the exception.
      code (int): Exception error code.

    """

Member Declaration

As with classes, their members are documented after declaration, indented to the appropriate level (always test!).

Function signatures can be documented as following:

        """Class methods are similar to regular functions.

        Note:
          Do not include the `self` parameter in the ``Args`` section.

        Args:
          param1: The first parameter.
          param2: The second parameter.

        Returns:
          True if successful, False otherwise.

        """

The same thing can be said for functions at the module level. Static module members can also be documented in the same way.

Symbolic Reference

Symbols can references between files in the following way:

URL Links

External Links:

`Docs for this project <http://packages.python.org/an_example_pypi_project/>`_

Anchor Declaration:

`Table of Contents`_

Images

.. figure::  images/sweat.jpg

Substitutions

Substitutions syntax is

.. |biohazard| image:: images/biohazard.png

Literal text replacement:

.. |doctest| replace:: :mod:`doctest`

The acceptable syntax can be found at sphinx.
